problems.title: How can one distinguish decentralized incentive-driven reproduction of power fro
problems.summary: How can one distinguish decentralized incentive-driven reproduction of power from explicit collusion, shared ideology, or simple selection effects in real cases? The conjecture is explanatory, but its
problems.full_text: How can one distinguish decentralized incentive-driven reproduction of power from explicit collusion, shared ideology, or simple selection effects in real cases? The conjecture is explanatory, but its strongest unresolved tension is empirical identifiability: what observable signatures would uniquely indicate that structure-induced repeated-game dynamics, rather than conspiracy or preference alignment, are doing the causal work?

problems.title: What transition mechanisms can actually move a power system from a domination-pr
problems.summary: What transition mechanisms can actually move a power system from a domination-preserving equilibrium to a fairer equilibrium without triggering collapse, backlash, or rapid re-lock-in? The conjecture
problems.full_text: What transition mechanisms can actually move a power system from a domination-preserving equilibrium to a fairer equilibrium without triggering collapse, backlash, or rapid re-lock-in? The conjecture says reform must change incentives, coordination, and first-mover risk, but it leaves open which interventions are sufficient, under what conditions, and how sequencing, scale, and institutional design affect success.
